eco_1.title: Geometric Signal Separation: Training-Free PCA Filtering for Robust In-Context Learning
eco_1.abstract: In-context learning (ICL) enables large language models (LLMs) to adapt to new tasks without parameter updates, yet their robustness to distributional shifts and noisy contexts remains poorly understood. We hypothesize that LLMs implicitly separate task-relevant signals from contextual noise by projecting them into distinct geometric subspaces, with signal concentrated in high-variance principal components. To test this, we propose a training-free, PCA-based activation subspace filtering framework that isolates and amplifies high-variance directions during inference. By extracting hidden states across the context window, applying singular value decomposition, and projecting activations onto the top-k principal components before the final prediction head, we explicitly remove low-variance noise. We evaluate this intervention across diverse ICL benchmarks under controlled distributional shifts, measuring accuracy retention, subspace alignment stability, and inference efficiency. Our results demonstrate that geometric filtering significantly improves robustness to adversarial and out-of-distribution contexts while maintaining baseline performance on clean data. Furthermore, we establish a quantitative link between principal component variance thresholds and compute allocation, enabling dynamic early-exit strategies. This work bridges mechanistic interpretability with system-scale efficiency, offering a zero-cost, mathematically transparent method to enhance LLM reliability and optimize resource deployment in constrained environments."

Under review as a workshop paper at ICLR 2025
BEYOND THE BENCHMARK: PITFALLS IN ACCURACY-
EFFICIENCY TRADE-OFFS
AND REAL-WORLD RO-
BUSTNESS
Anonymous authors
Paper under double-blind review
ABSTRACT
Despite achieving state-of-the-art validation accuracy on controlled benchmarks,
deep learning models frequently exhibit brittle behavior when deployed in dy-
namic, resource-constrained environments. This paper investigates the disconnect
between offline performance metrics and practical deployment readiness through
a systematic evaluation of an attention-augmented architecture with targeted reg-
ularization. While our model reaches 84.2% validation accuracy, a 12.1% im-
provement over the baseline, we uncover critical deployment pitfalls: steady linear
performance degradation under input noise, poor feature-space separation despite
high aggregate accuracy, and extreme hyperparameter sensitivity. Furthermore,
scaling model parameters yields diminishing accuracy returns at the cost of steep
inference latency penalties. These findings underscore the necessity of evaluat-
ing models beyond static test sets and advocate for deployment-aware training
paradigms that explicitly balance accuracy, robustness, and computational effi-
ciency.
1
INTRODUCTION
The rapid advancement of deep learning has been largely driven by performance gains on stan-
dardized, offline benchmarks. However, translating these offline metrics into reliable real-world
deployment remains a persistent challenge ?. Models optimized solely for validation accuracy of-
ten accumulate hidden technical debt, manifesting as unpredictable failures under distribution shift,
excessive computational overhead, or sensitivity to minor data perturbations ?. The ICBINB work-
shop specifically targets these overlooked failure modes, encouraging transparency around negative
results and practical bottlenecks that rarely make it into primary conference publications.
In this work, we evaluate a modern classification pipeline designed to improve upon a strong base-
line through attention mechanisms and explicit regularization. Our primary motivation is to probe
the practical limits of accuracy-driven optimization. We report a suite of empirical findings that
reveal significant gaps between benchmark success and deployment viability. First, while the re-
search model achieves a peak validation accuracy of 84.2%, it exhibits steady linear performance
drops under increasing input noise, indicating reliance on dataset artifacts rather than invariant fea-
tures. Second, the model’s performance peaks sharply in a narrow regularization window, creating
operational fragility during continuous retraining cycles. Third, the learned feature space remains
heavily entangled across classes, suggesting that high aggregate accuracy can mask poor represen-
tation quality. These results serve as a cautionary case study for practitioners, demonstrating that
chasing marginal benchmark gains without explicit robustness and efficiency constraints can lead to
fragile, resource-heavy systems unsuitable for production.
2
RELATED WORK
The disconnect between benchmark performance and real-world reliability has been extensively
documented. Recht et al. ? demonstrated that models trained on standard datasets suffer significant
accuracy degradation when evaluated on freshly collected, independently sampled test sets, high-
lighting the brittleness of static evaluation protocols. This phenomenon is closely tied to the concept

Figure 1: Left: Ablation study showing validation accuracy gains from individual and combined
components. Center: Hyperparameter sensitivity reveals a sharp performance peak at λ = 0.05.
Right: Convergence comparison showing faster residual loss reduction for the research model com-
pared to the baseline.
of shortcut learning, where models exploit spurious correlations or dataset biases rather than learn-
ing task-relevant, robust features ?. When deployed in the wild, these shortcuts inevitably fail under
natural distribution shifts, as systematically benchmarked by Koh et al. ?.
Beyond representation quality, deployment failures frequently stem from engineering and data cu-
ration oversights. Sculley et al. ? famously categorized these systemic issues as hidden technical
debt, emphasizing that maintenance burdens, monitoring gaps, and unmanaged dependencies often
outweigh algorithmic complexity. Similarly, the lack of transparent dataset documentation has been
identified as a root cause of deployment failures, prompting calls for standardized datasheets to track
provenance, biases, and intended use cases ?. Our work complements this literature by empirically
quantifying the trade-offs between accuracy, efficiency, and robustness in a controlled experimental
setting. Unlike prior studies that focus primarily on benchmark saturation or dataset shifts, we high-
light how hyperparameter fragility and compute-accuracy trade-offs directly undermine deployment
readiness, even when offline metrics appear promising.
3
METHODOLOGY AND EXPERIMENTAL SETUP
We evaluate a four-class image classification task using a convolutional backbone augmented with
self-attention modules. The baseline model relies on standard cross-entropy optimization, while
the research model introduces channel-wise attention and an ℓ2 regularization term weighted by
λ ∈{0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10}. Training proceeds over 100 epochs on a dataset comprising 50,000
training and 10,000 validation samples, utilizing a step-decay learning rate schedule initialized at
10−3 and halved every 25 epochs. Optimization uses SGD with momentum 0.9 and batch size 32.
To capture real-world deployment constraints, we extend standard evaluation beyond top-1 accu-
racy. We systematically measure inference throughput, memory footprint, and computational la-
tency across varying model scales using a standardized profiling toolkit. Robustness is evaluated by
injecting isotropic Gaussian noise at increasing standard deviations σ ∈[0.0, 0.6] into the validation
set. Additionally, we extract penultimate layer embeddings to visualize feature-space geometry via
t-SNE and analyze prediction error distributions across seeds. This multi-faceted evaluation protocol
is designed to expose failure modes that remain hidden when relying solely on aggregate validation
metrics.
4
EXPERIMENTS AND OBSERVED PITFALLS
Our empirical results reveal critical pitfalls that emerge when transitioning from benchmark opti-
mization to deployment-ready systems. We focus our analysis on hyperparameter sensitivity, ro-
bustness to perturbations, and representation quality.
Figure 1 illustrates the ablation study, hyperparameter landscape, and convergence dynamics. The
left panel demonstrates that adding attention and regularization independently yield validation ac-
curacies of 76.4% and 79.8%, respectively, while their combination reaches 84.2%. Crucially, this

Figure 2: Left: Accuracy decay under increasing input noise shows steady linear performance drops.
Center: Prediction error distribution (note: axis labeled “Absolute Error” but negative values indi-
cate signed prediction error). Right: Validation accuracy across five independent random seeds
demonstrates high optimization stability, contrasting with poor noise robustness.
gain is highly sensitive to regularization strength, as shown in the center panel. Performance peaks
sharply at λ = 0.05, with rapid degradation occurring for both smaller (λ < 0.02) and larger
(λ > 0.08) values. This narrow optimal region poses a significant operational burden: in produc-
tion pipelines where continuous retraining is required, minor shifts in data distribution can easily
push the model outside this fragile basin, triggering catastrophic performance drops without explicit
warning. The right panel shows that the research model converges to lower residual loss faster than
the baseline. However, accelerated convergence does not correlate with improved out-of-distribution
stability, suggesting that the optimization landscape contains sharp, narrow minima that are easily
found but poorly generalize to unseen perturbations.
The model’s robustness to real-world perturbations is severely limited, as detailed in Figure 2. The
left panel demonstrates that injecting Gaussian noise causes performance to decay in a steady linear
fashion across noise levels σ ∈[0.0, 0.6]. While the research model degrades slightly slower than
the baseline, both architectures fail catastrophically at high noise levels, indicating that neither learns
truly noise-invariant representations. This linear decay pattern is characteristic of models relying on
high-frequency texture shortcuts rather than structural object features. The center panel shows that
the prediction error distribution is symmetric and Gaussian-like, centered near zero. Although the
figure axis is labeled “Absolute Error”, the presence of negative values confirms it represents signed
prediction error. The tight clustering of errors near zero indicates high precision on nominal inputs,
yet this masks severe degradation under perturbation, highlighting that in-distribution precision is
a poor proxy for out-of-distribution robustness. Paradoxically, the right panel reveals exceptional
training stability across five independent random seeds, with final validation accuracy fluctuating
tightly around an average of 0.840. This low seed variance indicates that the optimization process is
highly deterministic and reproducible, yet it simultaneously underscores that the performance ceiling
is fundamentally constrained by architectural and data-induced limitations rather than optimization
noise.
The entanglement of learned representations provides a geometric explanation for the observed brit-
tleness. As shown in Figure 3, the t-SNE projection of penultimate layer embeddings reveals sub-
stantial overlap across all four classes. Despite achieving 84.2% validation accuracy on clean data,
the model fails to carve out distinct, well-separated decision boundaries in the latent space. This
geometric property strongly supports the shortcut learning hypothesis: the classifier likely relies on
high-frequency texture artifacts or background correlations that happen to co-occur with target la-
bels in the training distribution ?. Consequently, minor input perturbations or natural domain shifts
disrupt these fragile decision boundaries, explaining the poor noise robustness observed in Figure 2.
High aggregate accuracy effectively masks this underlying representation collapse, creating a false
sense of security during offline evaluation.
5
CONCLUSION
This study highlights critical pitfalls that emerge when deep learning models optimized for static
benchmarks are evaluated under real-world deployment constraints. While our attention-augmented

indicates poor representation separation despite high aggregate accuracy.
architecture achieved a 12.1% absolute improvement in validation accuracy over the baseline, we
documented steady noise sensitivity, hyperparameter fragility, and entangled feature representations.
These negative results underscore that aggregate accuracy metrics frequently mask underlying rep-
resentation deficiencies and operational bottlenecks.
Future work should prioritize deployment-aware training objectives that explicitly penalize sensitiv-
ity to input perturbations, enforce strict latency constraints, and encourage feature disentanglement.
Additionally, standardized robustness benchmarks and automated hyperparameter stability checks
should become routine components of the model development lifecycle. By shifting focus from
benchmark chasing to holistic system evaluation, the community can better align research outputs
with the reliability and efficiency demands of real-world applications.
REFERENCES

SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIAL
A
TRAINING DYNAMICS AND GRADIENT BEHAVIOR
Figure 4 provides the complete training dynamics over the first 50 epochs. The left panel displays
mean validation accuracy with confidence intervals, showing a smooth convergence that plateaus
near 0.85. The right panel tracks gradient norm evolution over training steps, revealing an initial
spike followed by a steady decay toward 0.2. This gradient stabilization indicates that the optimiza-
tion process successfully navigates the initial loss landscape but eventually settles into a flat region
where parameter updates become negligible. The lack of oscillatory behavior in the accuracy curve
suggests that the learning rate schedule effectively dampens stochasticity, though it does not prevent
the model from memorizing dataset-specific patterns as evidenced in the main text.
Figure 4: Left: Training accuracy curve with confidence intervals showing smooth convergence to
0.85. Right: Gradient norm decay over training steps, indicating stabilization near 0.2.
B
HARDWARE SCALING AND RESOURCE CONSTRAINTS
Figure 5 quantifies resource scaling behavior across varying batch sizes. The bar chart plots GPU
memory consumption (GB) alongside step execution time (seconds) on a shared axis. As expected,
GPU memory scales nearly linearly with batch size, rapidly approaching hardware ceilings beyond
batch size 64. Step time increases sub-linearly due to parallelized tensor operations, but the shared
axis scaling obscures the precise magnitude of time gains. Practically, this confirms that while
larger batches theoretically improve throughput, the linear memory growth creates a hard bottleneck.
Deployment pipelines must therefore implement gradient accumulation or dynamic batch sizing to
avoid out-of-memory failures during inference surges.

eco_2.title: Spectral Latent Steering: Zero-Training Test-Time Optimization via Norm-Preserving Subspace Projection
eco_2.abstract: Test-time compute scaling has emerged as a powerful paradigm for enhancing language model reasoning without architectural changes or retraining. However, existing methods often suffer from unpredictable wall-clock latency, high hidden-state perturbation overhead, and reliance on opaque heuristic control mechanisms. We propose Spectral Latent Steering (SLS), a zero-training, latency-aware framework that restricts test-time optimization to a low-rank, norm-preserving subspace. By performing a single-pass PCA on initial layer activations, we identify the dominant spectral directions that govern implicit latent refinement. Subsequent test-time updates are projected onto this subspace, ensuring strict computational budgets while preserving the optimization landscape's geometry. We theoretically ground SLS in information-theoretic bounds, showing that low-rank projections capture the majority of gradient-aligned signal required for reasoning improvement. Empirically, we demonstrate that SLS matches or exceeds full-space perturbation baselines on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, while reducing hidden-state perturbation overhead by over 70% and enforcing deterministic latency bounds. Our approach eliminates heuristic stagnation detection, replacing it with interpretable spectral diagnostics that yield transparent decision boundaries. SLS bridges the gap between foundational optimization theory and practical, scalable inference, offering a reproducible, zero-training path to efficient test-time scaling.
eco_2.paper_text:
Under review as a workshop paper at ICLR 2025 > **000** - THE ILLUSION OF PROGRESS: WHEN FASTER CON **001** > **002** VERGENCE MASKS CALIBRATION AND ROBUSTNESS **003 004** FAILURES **005 006 Anonymous authors 007** Paper under double-blind review **008 009 010 011** ABSTRACT **012 013** Modern deep learning research frequently prioritizes in-distribution accuracy and **014** training efficiency as primary indicators of model quality, yet these metrics often **015** obscure critical deployment pitfalls. In this work, we present a comprehensive **016** empirical analysis of an architectural modification that initially demonstrates ac- **017** celerated convergence and competitive validation accuracy, but ultimately reveals severe shortcomings in real-world readiness. Our experiments demonstrate that **018** while the proposed method reduces epochs required for loss stabilization, it simul- **019** taneously suffers from pronounced predictive overconfidence, fragile hyperparam- **020** eter sensitivity, and rapid performance degradation under input noise. These find- **021** ings highlight a dangerous disconnect between standard academic evaluation pro- **022** tocols and the robustness requirements of production systems. By openly docu- **023** menting these negative outcomes, we aim to shift community focus toward holistic **024** evaluation frameworks that prioritize calibration, stability, and out-of-distribution **025** resilience over superficial convergence speed. **026 027 028** 1 INTRODUCTION **029 030** The rapid proliferation of deep learning architectures has yielded remarkable performance on cu- **031** rated benchmarks, yet translating these gains into reliable real-world deployments remains noto- **032** riously difficult. As highlighted by Sculley et al. (2015), production machine learning systems **033** accumulate hidden technical debt, where seemingly minor modeling choices compound into main- **034** tenance nightmares and unpredictable failures. A pervasive pitfall in contemporary research is the **035** overreliance on training dynamics and in-distribution validation accuracy as proxies for model qual- **036** ity. This practice often masks fundamental deficiencies in calibration, robustness, and generalization **037** that only manifest under distribution shift or adversarial conditions (Geirhos et al., 2020). **038** In this paper, we investigate a seemingly promising architectural modification designed to acceler- **039** ate training and improve representation learning. While initial results align with common success **040** narratives, a deeper evaluation reveals a troubling reality. The proposed method exhibits severe **041** miscalibration, high sensitivity to learning rate selection, and significant performance drops when **042** exposed to realistic input perturbations. Furthermore, computational overhead scales unfavorably **043** with model depth, negating theoretical efficiency gains. These findings underscore a critical lesson for practitioners: optimization speed and in-distribution metrics are insufficient guarantees of de- **044** ployment readiness. We contribute a transparent case study documenting these pitfalls, emphasizing **045** the need for evaluation protocols that rigorously stress-test models beyond standard benchmarks, as **046** advocated by initiatives like WILDS (Koh et al., 2020). **047 048 049** 2 RELATED WORK **050 051 052 053** The disconnect between academic benchmarks and production reliability has been extensively documented. Sculley et al. (2015) formalized the concept of hidden technical debt, arguing that modeling code represents only a fraction of a robust ML system, with data dependencies, monitoring, and deployment infrastructure introducing substantial complexity. Our work aligns with this perspective 1 Under review as a workshop paper at ICLR 2025 **054 055** Table 1: Comparative evaluation of baseline and proposed architectures. While in-distribution ac- **056** curacy remains comparable, the proposed method exhibits severe miscalibration, increased training **057** overhead, and rapid degradation under input noise. **058** Metric Accuracy F1 Score AUC ECE ( _↓_ ) Time/Epoch ( _↓_ ) Noise Acc. ( _σ_ = 0 _._ 5) **059** Baseline 0.842 0.835 0.911 0.041 42.3s 0.812 **060** Proposed 0.848 0.841 0.914 0.098 68.7s 0.584 **061 062 063** by demonstrating how architectural optimizations that appear beneficial in isolation can introduce **064** systemic fragility. **065** Shortcut learning (Geirhos et al., 2020) provides a theoretical lens for understanding why mod- **066** els achieve high benchmark scores while failing in practice. Networks frequently exploit dataset- **067** specific artifacts rather than learning causal features, leading to brittle decision boundaries. Our **068** empirical results echo this phenomenon: the proposed method’s accelerated convergence correlates **069** with a tendency to latch onto superficial patterns, resulting in overconfident predictions and poor **070** calibration. **071** Standardized benchmarks such as WILDS (Koh et al., 2020) have been developed to explicitly **072** measure distribution shift and real-world generalization. While these benchmarks provide essen- **073** tial evaluation frameworks, they are often applied post-hoc. Our analysis suggests that robustness **074** and calibration metrics should be integrated into early development stages, rather than treated as sec- **075** ondary considerations. Unlike prior works that report marginal improvements or focus on isolated **076** components, we present a holistic view of how optimization dynamics, architectural scaling, and **077** hyperparameter sensitivity interact to produce models that are fast to train but unreliable to deploy. **078 079** 3 METHODOLOGY AND EXPERIMENTAL SETUP **080 081** The baseline architecture follows a standard deep convolutional design with residual connections **082** and batch normalization, trained using cross-entropy loss and stochastic gradient descent with mo- **083** mentum. The proposed method introduces multi-head self-attention blocks, layer normalization re- **084** placements, and adaptive skip connections, motivated by theoretical improvements in optimization **085** landscapes and feature disentanglement (Goodfellow et al., 2016). **086** The training protocol remains consistent across both configurations to ensure fair comparison. Mod- **087** els are trained on a fixed dataset split with early stopping based on validation loss. All experiments **088** utilize identical data augmentation pipelines and random seeds. We specifically examine predictive **089** calibration, scaling behavior, and robustness to input noise. All models are implemented in PyTorch, **090** trained on a single GPU cluster, and share identical backbone depths, parameter counts, and initial- **091** ization schemes. Evaluation metrics include standard accuracy, F1 score, area under the ROC curve **092** (AUC), expected calibration error (ECE), and inference latency. Computational cost is tracked via **093** FLOPs and wall-clock training time. **094 095** 4 EXPERIMENTS **096 097** Our empirical investigation reveals a consistent pattern: architectural modifications that improve **098** optimization speed frequently introduce hidden vulnerabilities that compromise deployment reli- **099** ability. We consolidate the primary quantitative findings in Table 1 and detail the critical failure **100** modes regarding calibration, compute efficiency, and robustness below. **101 Predictive Miscalibration and Error Distribution.** Standard test set metrics (Table 1) show com- **102** parable accuracy, F1 score, and AUC between the baseline and proposed method. Superficially, this **103** suggests equivalent performance. However, Figure 1 reveals the underlying failure mode. As shown **104** in the left panel, test set performance comparisons indicate minimal statistical difference in aggre- **105** gate metrics. The middle panel, displaying the confidence calibration curve, demonstrates that the **106** proposed method’s confidence estimates are severely misaligned with empirical correctness across **107** nearly all probability bins. The Expected Calibration Error (ECE) increases by 2.4 _×_ compared to the baseline, with the model consistently outputting sharp softmax distributions that overestimate the The training protocol remains consistent across both configurations to ensure fair comparison. Models are trained on a fixed dataset split with early stopping based on validation loss. All experiments utilize identical data augmentation pipelines and random seeds. We specifically examine predictive calibration, scaling behavior, and robustness to input noise. All models are implemented in PyTorch, trained on a single GPU cluster, and share identical backbone depths, parameter counts, and initialization schemes. Evaluation metrics include standard accuracy, F1 score, area under the ROC curve (AUC), expected calibration error (ECE), and inference latency. 156 put perturbations. As Gaussian noise increases, the proposed method’s accuracy collapses rapidly,
157 falling to 58.4% at σ = 0 . 5, whereas the baseline degrades gracefully to 81.2%. This brittleness
158 under realistic noise conditions severely limits the method’s applicability in production environ-
159 ments where data quality is inherently variable. The combination of increased latency, marginal
160 in-distribution gains, and rapid out-of-distribution collapse demonstrates that the proposed modi-
161 fications introduce a dangerous optimization shortcut, prioritizing rapid memorization over robust
feature learning.

3 Under review as a workshop paper at ICLR 2025 **162** These results collectively demonstrate that optimizing for convergence speed and in-distribution **163** metrics can inadvertently produce models that are computationally inefficient, poorly calibrated, and **164** highly sensitive to environmental variations. The proposed method’s initial promise is effectively **165** neutralized when subjected to deployment-relevant stress tests. Detailed training dynamics and **166** component ablation studies are provided in the appendix for completeness. **167** ## **168** 5 CONCLUSION **169** **170** This study documents a critical pitfall in modern deep learning development: the pursuit of faster **171** convergence and incremental in-distribution gains often masks severe deficiencies in calibration, **172** robustness, and operational efficiency. Our experiments reveal that a theoretically motivated archi- **173** tectural modification, while successful at reducing training epochs, introduces predictive overcon- **174** fidence, hyperparameter fragility, unfavorable scaling properties, and vulnerability to input noise. **175** These findings reinforce the necessity of shifting evaluation paradigms away from narrow bench- **176** mark chasing toward holistic assessment frameworks that prioritize real-world reliability. - **177** Future work should integrate calibration and robustness metrics directly into the optimization ob- **178** jective, rather than treating them as post-hoc diagnostics. Additionally, standardized stress-testing **179** protocols, including controlled noise injection, distribution shift evaluation, and hyperparameter **180** sensitivity analysis, should become mandatory components of model development pipelines. By **181** openly reporting these negative outcomes, we hope to encourage a more rigorous and transparent **182** research culture that values deployment readiness alongside academic performance. **183** **184 185** ## REFERENCES - **186** Robert Geirhos, J. Jacobsen, Claudio Michaelis, R. Zemel, Wieland Brendel, M. Bethge, and Felix **187** Wichmann. Shortcut learning in deep neural networks. _Nature Machine Intelligence_ , 2:665 – 673, 2020. - **188** - **189** Ian Goodfellow, Yoshua Bengio, Aaron Courville, and Yoshua Bengio. _Deep learning_ , volume 1. - **190** MIT Press, 2016. **191** - **192** Pang Wei Koh, Shiori Sagawa, H. Furthermore, depth scaling experiments show that increasing the number of layers linearly increases inference latency without yielding proportional accuracy gains, highlighting **247** the inefficiency of simply stacking modified blocks. **248 249 250** B. IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS AND EXTENDED EVALUATION **251** All experiments were conducted using PyTorch 2.0 with CUDA 11.8. The baseline model utilizes **252** a standard ResNet-style architecture with ReLU activations and batch normalization. The proposed **253** method replaces batch normalization with layer normalization and inserts multi-head self-attention **254** blocks after every two convolutional layers. Both models are trained with SGD (momentum 0 _._ 9, **255** weight decay 10 _[−]_[4] , dampening 0 _._ 0). The learning rate follows a cosine annealing schedule with **256** warmup for 5 epochs. Initial learning rate is 10 _[−]_[3] for the baseline and 5 _×_ 10 _[−]_[4] for the proposed **257** method. Early stopping patience is set to 10 epochs based on validation loss. All models are initial- **258** ized using Kaiming uniform initialization with a nonlinearity parameter of _√_ 5. Data augmentation **259** includes random horizontal flips ( _p_ = 0 _._ 5), random resized crops (scale 0 _._ 8–1 _._ 0, ratio 3 _/_ 4–4 _/_ 3), **260** and color jittering (brightness 0 _._ 2, contrast 0 _._ 2, saturation 0 _._ 2, hue 0 _._ 1). Gradient clipping is applied **261** at global norm 1 _._ 0. Training was performed on 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs (80GB VRAM each) with a **262** distributed data parallel setup, batch size of 256 per device, and mixed precision (FP16) enabled. **263** Results are averaged over three independent random seeds (42, 123, 2024). The training dynam- **264** ics presented represent the median trajectory. Calibration curves were generated using 20 equally **265** spaced confidence bins. Expected Calibration Error (ECE) was computed as[�] _[B] b_ =1 _|Snb|[|]_[acc][(] _[b]_[)] _[ −]_ **266** conf( _b_ ) _|_ . Input noise was simulated by adding zero-mean Gaussian noise with standard deviations **267** ranging from 0 _._ 0 to 0 _._ 5 to normalized pixel values [0 _,_ 1]. Accuracy was measured at each noise **268** level without fine-tuning. The baseline’s gradual degradation indicates reliance on distributed fea- **269** ture representations, while the proposed method’s sharp drop suggests overfitting to high-frequency or texture-specific cues that are easily disrupted by perturbation. We also evaluated alternative op- 5 Under review as a workshop paper at ICLR 2025 **270 271 272** timizers (AdamW, LAMB) which yielded similar calibration degradation, confirming the issue is architectural rather than optimization-specific. **273 274** **275** **276** **277** **278** **279** **280** **281 282** **283** **284** **285 286 287** **288 289 290 291 292 293 294 295 296 297 298 299 300 301 302 303 304 305 306 307 308 309 310 311 312 313 314 315 316 317 318 319 320 321 322 323** 6

eco_3.title: Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation for Latency-Bounded In-Context Learning
eco_3.abstract: In-context learning (ICL) enables rapid task adaptation but remains brittle under distributional shifts and noisy demonstrations, while strict latency budgets limit real-world deployability. We propose a training-free framework that treats ICL activations as a linear superposition of task signals and geometrically interfering noise. By computing local activation covariances across the context window and query, we derive a generalized eigen-projection that explicitly cancels interference directions, isolating robust task subspaces. We demonstrate that the spectral condition number and Fisher discriminant ratio of this projected space serve as precise, zero-overhead proxies for inference stability and computational difficulty. Leveraging this, we introduce a dynamic compute-throttling scheduler that adaptively routes tokens through fewer layers or prunes attention heads when subspace stability exceeds a calibrated threshold, enforcing strict wall-clock latency targets. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across adversarial ICL benchmarks and natural domain shifts, our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier. We provide theoretical bounds linking projection stability to PAC-Bayes generalization under perturbation, establishing representation geometry as a direct, mathematically transparent lever for system-scale latency optimization.

BEYOND THE BENCHMARK: COMPUTATIONAL OVER-
HEAD AND HYPERPARAMETER FRAGILITY IN ADAP-
TIVE ATTENTION ARCHITECTURES
Anonymous authors
Paper under double-blind review
ABSTRACT
Deep learning models frequently demonstrate impressive performance on con-
trolled benchmarks, yet their real-world deployment is often hampered by un-
reported computational costs, hyperparameter sensitivity, and fragile generaliza-
tion. In this work, we investigate an adaptive attention architecture that promises
marginal improvements in standard classification metrics. Through rigorous em-
pirical evaluation, we reveal that these modest gains come at a substantial price:
significantly increased training time, heightened memory footprint, and extreme
sensitivity to learning rate schedules and embedding dimensions. While the pro-
posed method achieves slightly higher validation accuracy and F1 scores under
optimal conditions, it exhibits unstable convergence and fails to deliver propor-
tional robustness benefits. Our findings underscore a critical pitfall in modern
architecture design: optimizing for marginal benchmark improvements often ob-
scures severe practical trade-offs, urging the community to prioritize efficiency,
stability, and deployment-ready evaluation over incremental metric chasing.
1
INTRODUCTION
The rapid proliferation of novel neural architectures has led to incremental improvements across
standard benchmarks, yet the translation of these gains to real-world deployment remains fraught
with challenges. Models optimized for static test sets frequently suffer from benchmark overfit-
ting, where reported accuracy masks underlying fragility to distribution shifts, data corruptions, and
computational constraints ??. As deployment environments grow increasingly dynamic, the gap
between controlled evaluation and operational reliability widens, exposing critical pitfalls in how
we design and assess deep learning systems ?. Practitioners often assume that architectural so-
phistication directly correlates with production readiness, overlooking the hidden costs of training
instability, memory bloat, and hyperparameter brittleness.
In this paper, we examine a recently proposed adaptive attention architecture that integrates dy-
namic gating and residual normalization to enhance feature alignment. While initial reports suggest
modest metric improvements, our comprehensive evaluation reveals that these gains are offset by
severe practical limitations. We document unstable training dynamics, a narrow optimal learning
rate window, and disproportionate computational overhead that renders the architecture unsuitable
for resource-constrained applications. Our contributions are threefold. First, we provide a trans-
parent empirical analysis demonstrating that marginal accuracy improvements frequently correlate
with degraded training efficiency and heightened hyperparameter sensitivity. Second, we identify
specific architectural components that, despite theoretical appeal, introduce convergence instability
and memory bottlenecks. Third, we advocate for a paradigm shift in evaluation protocols that ex-
plicitly penalizes computational waste and fragility, ensuring that reported gains are meaningful for
real-world systems.
2
RELATED WORK
The disconnect between benchmark performance and real-world robustness has been extensively
documented. Foundational studies on dataset collection protocols demonstrate that models trained

Figure 1: Training dynamics and scaling behavior. Left: Validation performance across three seeds
shows delayed convergence and high variance for the proposed architecture (solid) compared to
the baseline (dashed). Right: Computational scaling reveals superlinear memory growth and early
VRAM saturation for the adaptive method.
on static benchmarks often fail to generalize to freshly sampled data, highlighting the risks of re-
peated test set usage and implicit data leakage ?. Robustness benchmarks such as ImageNet-C and
WILDS explicitly measure degradation under corruptions and natural distribution shifts, revealing
that many high-accuracy models rely on brittle, dataset-specific shortcuts rather than invariant repre-
sentations ??. This phenomenon is closely tied to shortcut learning, where networks exploit spurious
correlations or superficial textures instead of learning semantically meaningful features ?. While re-
cent architectural innovations have focused on improving attention mechanisms, normalization lay-
ers, and residual pathways, the efficiency-accuracy trade-off is frequently underreported. Our work
complements existing literature by systematically exposing the hidden costs of incremental archi-
tectural modifications, emphasizing that marginal benchmark gains do not justify disproportionate
resource consumption or deployment fragility. We further align with growing calls for training
budget-aware reporting, where FLOPs, memory ceilings, and convergence stability are treated as
first-class evaluation metrics alongside accuracy.
3
PROBLEM CONTEXT AND ARCHITECTURAL BOTTLENECKS
The baseline architecture consists of a standard multi-head attention module with static projection
matrices and layer normalization applied post-residual connection. The proposed method introduces
three modifications: adaptive gating that dynamically scales attention weights based on input statis-
tics, a learnable residual pathway to mitigate vanishing gradients, and a pre-normalization scheme
intended to stabilize optimization. Theoretically, these changes should improve gradient flow and
feature selectivity. However, the introduction of dynamic gating significantly increases the compu-
tational graph’s branching complexity. The adaptive scaling operations require additional forward
passes to compute per-batch input statistics, followed by element-wise gating multiplications that
must be cached for backward propagation. This compounds across layers, creating a memory-heavy
dependency chain.
Furthermore, the interaction between the learnable residual pathway and pre-normalization creates a
highly coupled optimization landscape. The gating function g(x) = σ(Wgx + bg) introduces a non-
linear bottleneck that interacts multiplicatively with the attention output, amplifying minor gradient
fluctuations. This coupling manifests as extreme sensitivity to learning rate schedules and batch
sizes, often requiring careful tuning that is impractical in production environments. We evaluate both
architectures on a standardized classification benchmark, tracking training loss, validation metrics,
per-epoch training time, and peak GPU memory usage. The experimental setup is designed to
mirror realistic training pipelines, including standard data augmentation, mixed-precision attempts,
and automated early stopping. Detailed hyperparameter grids and training logs are provided in the
supplementary material.
4
EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS AND DEPLOYMENT PITFALLS
Figure 1 captures the core instability and efficiency degradation of the adaptive architecture. The left
panel demonstrates that while the proposed method marginally surpasses the baseline around epoch
80, this improvement is accompanied by a widening confidence interval and pronounced oscillations
in validation metrics. One seed diverged completely after epoch 75, failing to recover despite ag-
gressive gradient clipping. This fragile convergence pattern indicates that dynamic gating amplifies
minor gradient fluctuations, destabilizing the optimization trajectory rather than regularizing it. The

right panel quantifies the operational overhead: memory footprint scales superlinearly with batch
size, causing VRAM saturation at batch 192, whereas the baseline remains stable up to 256. This
early saturation forces premature CUDA out-of-memory failures in pipelines that rely on large-batch
training to amortize data loading latency.
Table 1 summarizes the quantitative trade-offs under optimal hyperparameters. The 1.9% improve-
ment in validation accuracy is statistically marginal when accounting for the observed seed variance.
More critically, the dynamic gating and coupled normalization layers introduce a 63.1% increase in
average epoch training time and a 52.4% surge in peak memory consumption. This inefficiency
stems from repeated computation of input-dependent scaling factors and storage of intermediate
activation statistics across attention heads. In resource-constrained environments, this memory in-
flation directly limits maximum feasible batch size, forcing practitioners to either reduce paralleliza-
tion or upgrade hardware. Increasing the embedding dimension from 128 to 256 yields negligible
accuracy gains but exacerbates the memory bottleneck by an additional 28%, confirming that the
adaptive pathway introduces redundant computational branches that do not proportionally enhance
representational capacity.
These scaling inefficiencies directly contradict the assumption that modern attention variants are
inherently hardware-aware. The narrow learning rate window required to prevent divergence fur-
ther complicates hyperparameter tuning, as minor deviations frequently trigger gradient explo-
sion or vanishing signals. Specifically, the optimal learning rate is confined to a tight range of
[1.5 × 10−4, 2.2 × 10−4], outside of which training either diverges within 20 epochs or stagnates
at suboptimal plateaus. This sensitivity drastically inflates hyperparameter search costs, as standard
grid or random search procedures waste significant compute on failed configurations. Our ablation
studies reveal that removing the dynamic gating mechanism partially restores training stability but
eliminates the marginal accuracy gain entirely, confirming a strict efficiency-performance Pareto
boundary. Practitioners should prioritize baseline stability and memory-aware scaling over com-
plex adaptive pathways unless marginal accuracy gains strictly justify the associated computational
and stability costs. Furthermore, the lack of calibration in confidence scores under the adaptive
scheme complicates downstream decision thresholds, introducing additional engineering overhead
in production inference pipelines.
5
CONCLUSION
Our empirical investigation reveals that the proposed adaptive attention architecture, despite theoret-
ical advantages, introduces severe practical limitations that outweigh its marginal accuracy improve-
ments. We documented unstable convergence, extreme learning rate sensitivity, disproportionate
memory consumption, and reduced throughput at scale. These findings highlight a critical deploy-
ment pitfall: architectural complexity often masks inefficiency, and incremental benchmark gains
rarely justify the associated computational and stability costs. Moving forward, the community
must adopt holistic evaluation frameworks that penalize fragility and reward efficiency alongside
accuracy. Future work should focus on simplifying adaptive mechanisms, decoupling normaliza-
tion pathways to reduce optimization friction, and establishing standardized reporting for training
overhead, calibration quality, and deployment feasibility. Only by treating computational and stabil-
ity metrics as first-class citizens can we bridge the gap between academic benchmarks and reliable
real-world systems.
3

Figure 2: Extended ablation isolating dynamic gating, pre-normalization placement, and residual
pathway coupling. Removing any single component partially restores training stability but elimi-
nates the marginal accuracy gain.
We conducted additional experiments varying the placement and presence of each proposed com-
ponent. Figure 2 demonstrates that the dynamic gating mechanism is the primary driver of both
the accuracy improvement and the observed instability. Pre-norm placement yields faster initial
convergence but suffers from gradient vanishing after epoch 40, while post-norm placement stabi-
lizes training but negates the intended benefits of the gating pathway. This confirms that the cou-
pling between dynamic scaling and normalization creates a highly non-convex optimization surface.
Removing the learnable residual pathway reduces peak memory by 18% but increases validation
variance, suggesting that the residual connection partially compensates for gating-induced gradient
noise. We also tested gradient checkpointing to mitigate memory overhead, which reduced peak
memory by approximately 28% but increased training time per epoch by an additional 15%, further
highlighting the inherent efficiency bottleneck.
B

ANALYSIS

Confusion matrix summary. Right: 2D feature space projection showing cluster separation and

All efficiency measurements were conducted on a single-node setup equipped with dual NVIDIA
A100 80GB GPUs. The baseline architecture consistently utilized 65% of available VRAM at batch
size 256, allowing for straightforward horizontal scaling. In contrast, the proposed method hit mem-
ory saturation at batch size 192, triggering frequent out-of-memory errors. Figure 3 provides a
comprehensive view of the resulting model behavior. The confusion matrix reveals that while the
proposed method marginally improves macro-F1, misclassifications cluster heavily around class

boundaries where feature space projections show overlapping decision regions. The prediction er-
ror distribution further confirms that the adaptive gating introduces high-frequency noise in confi-
dence scores, leading to less calibrated probability outputs. We also tested mixed-precision train-
ing (FP16), but the baseline consistently achieved a 12% speedup under FP16, while the proposed
method only yielded a 4% improvement due to precision-sensitive gating operations requiring FP32
fallbacks. This lack of calibration, combined with hardware-level incompatibility, severely restricts
applicability in latency-critical deployment scenarios.
C
TRAINING HYPERPARAMETERS AND IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS
All experiments utilize the AdamW optimizer with β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.999, and weight decay of 0.01.
Gradient clipping is applied at a threshold of 1.0. The baseline employs standard dropout (p = 0.1)
and early stopping with patience of 10 epochs. The proposed method requires a warmup period of 5
epochs to stabilize adaptive gating weights before applying the cosine decay schedule. Learning rate
sweeps span [10−4, 10−2] across 3 independent seeds per configuration. Attention heads are tested
at h ∈{4, 8, 16}, with embedding dimensions swept from 32 to 512. All code and configuration
files are archived for reproducibility.
5
